   • I respect his depth on global macro and banking, but I think he’s ideologically biased against crypto. I’d give his Bitcoin views low credibility—even though his economic warnings are usually well-grounded, he’s openly dismissive of any digital-asset upside.  

Cathie Wood

I’d say she’s a credible voice on technology adoption curves but her $500K price target feels like a high-risk, high-reward forecast more than a consensus estimate. I trust her on long-term tech disruption, but I’d temper her Bitcoin number with other views.  

Jamie Dimon  
1. Do you know who he is? yes  
2. What I know & how much I trust him:  
   • Longtime CEO of JPMorgan Chase, staunch critic of Bitcoin (“fraud” comment) though the bank quietly uses blockchain in operations. He’s deeply embedded in the traditional banking system.

Larry Fink

2. What I know & how much I trust him:

   • He sits at the center of institutional investing and has a more balanced, fiduciary perspective. His $150K projection signals that big institutions see serious upside. I find his estimate more credible than Roubini’s or Dimon’s—though I’d still weigh it alongside Cathie Wood’s optimism and broader market signals.

I respect that he’s built a business advising on precious metals, but I know his bearish position is also self-interested. 3. Peter Schiff  
   1. Do I know him? Vaguely.  
   2. Would I look him up? Yes—before giving his $3 K call any weight.  
      • Likely find: CEO of Euro Pacific Capital, gold-bug commentator, hosts a libertarian-leaning podcast, chronic Bitcoin critic.  
      • How it’d change my view: I’d see he’s philosophically committed to gold and anti-crypto, so his low target is driven by bias against the asset class. I’d notch down how seriously I take his number.  

4. Elon Musk  
   1. Do I know him? Yes.  
   2. What I know & how much I trust him:  
      • CEO of Tesla/SpaceX, very high-profile on social media, has swayed crypto prices with tweets.

In sum, I’d give the most calibrated weight to Dorsey (because of his stake in crypto infrastructure) and Roubini (as a bearish risk check), discount Schiff heavily for ideological bias, and view Musk’s number as an outlier driven by influence rather than deep financial modeling.

     • That context would make me take his forecast more seriously, since his firm is positioning institutional money into Bitcoin. I’d still discount some optimism (they want inflows), but I’d view $150K by 2025 as within the realm of credible institutional-demand scenarios.

     Yes.
